Q1.bottleneck: Sequential scan with simple aggregation - already well optimized with perfect hash aggregation
Q1.root_cause: Query executes successfully in 271ms. Performance is good but still 36% slower than DuckDB (199ms). Gap likely due to: (1) loading all 16 lineitem columns into memory instead of just the 7 needed, (2) lack of SIMD vectorization for scan and arithmetic, (3) no zone maps for date filtering.
Q1.benchmark_gap: 271ms vs DuckDB 199ms = 36% slower. Gap is small - Q1 optimization has low priority vs fixing Q3/Q6 crashes.

Q3.bottleneck: CRITICAL: Program crashes with std::bad_alloc before Q3 can execute
Q3.root_cause: Memory exhaustion. Peak memory 15.7 GB indicates the storage layer is loading enormous amounts of unused data. Root causes: (1) All 16 lineitem columns loaded (including l_comment VARCHAR(44) - 60M strings never used), (2) std::string overhead (24 bytes/string + data = ~6-8 GB for comment alone), (3) All 9 orders columns loaded (including o_comment VARCHAR(79) - 15M strings), (4) No lazy/on-demand column loading. The system tries to allocate ~20+ GB for full materialization but only has 16 GB available.
Q3.benchmark_gap: Infinite - cannot execute vs DuckDB 135ms. CRITICAL BLOCKER.

Q6.bottleneck: CRITICAL: Program crashes with std::bad_alloc before Q6 can execute
Q6.root_cause: Same memory exhaustion issue as Q3. Q6 only needs 4 lineitem columns (l_shipdate, l_discount, l_quantity, l_extendedprice) but all 16 are loaded. The ~20 GB memory footprint from loading unused string columns exceeds available RAM.
Q6.benchmark_gap: Infinite - cannot execute vs DuckDB 27ms. CRITICAL BLOCKER.

analysis.overall: CRITICAL MEMORY CRISIS: System crashes after Q1 due to loading all table columns into memory instead of only needed columns. Q1 works (271ms, good performance) but uses excessive memory. Q3 and Q6 cannot execute. Current implementation loads 100% of all table data (~20+ GB uncompressed) but queries only need 20-40% of columns. Primary bottleneck is storage layer design, NOT query execution algorithms. The perfect hash aggregation (Q1), bloom filters (Q3), and top-K heap (Q3) are well-implemented but irrelevant if queries cannot run due to OOM.

recommendations.description: CRITICAL FIX for memory crisis. Modify storage layer to load ONLY columns actually used by queries, not all columns. Changes: (1) In storage.h, add 'std::unordered_set<std::string> columns_to_load' parameter to load_lineitem/load_orders/load_customer functions. (2) In storage.cpp, skip push_back for columns not in the set (e.g., if columns_to_load.count('l_comment') == 0, don't load l_comment at all). (3) In main.cpp, call loaders with explicit column lists: load_lineitem(..., {'l_orderkey', 'l_quantity', 'l_extendedprice', 'l_discount', 'l_tax', 'l_returnflag', 'l_linestatus', 'l_shipdate'}) for Q1, load_lineitem(..., {'l_orderkey', 'l_extendedprice', 'l_discount', 'l_shipdate'}) for Q6, etc. This eliminates loading 60M copies of l_comment (VARCHAR(44)), l_shipinstruct (CHAR(25)), l_shipmode (CHAR(10)) which consume 6-8 GB alone. Expected memory reduction: 20 GB → 4-6 GB.
recommendations.expected_improvement: Eliminates OOM crash. Enables Q3 and Q6 to execute. Memory footprint reduction: 15.7 GB → 4-6 GB (70% reduction). Load time may improve 30-50% (less parsing). No query execution time impact - this is pure overhead elimination.

recommendations.technique: String interning for low-cardinality string columns
recommendations.description: After implementing selective loading, optimize remaining string columns with string interning/deduplication for low-cardinality columns. Target columns: c_mktsegment (5 distinct values, 1.5M rows = 300K reduction to 5 strings), o_orderpriority (5 distinct values, 15M rows), o_clerk (~1000 distinct, 15M rows). Implementation: (1) Create StringPool class (see string-optimization.md example) with intern() method. (2) In load_customer, replace 'table.c_mktsegment.push_back(fields[6])' with 'table.c_mktsegment.push_back(string_pool.intern(fields[6]))'. (3) Change c_mktsegment from std::vector<std::string> to std::vector<const char*>. (4) String comparisons become pointer equality (O(1) instead of O(n)). Expected savings: c_mktsegment alone saves ~1.5M × 24 bytes (std::string overhead) = 36 MB, plus deduplication of actual string data (10 bytes × 1.5M = 15 MB) → 51 MB total per table. For Q3, c_mktsegment='BUILDING' filter becomes pointer comparison instead of string comparison.
recommendations.expected_improvement: Memory: 100-200 MB reduction across customer/orders tables. Q3 customer filter speedup: 5-10x (pointer equality vs strcmp). Load time: 10-20% faster (deduplicated strings). Low risk - isolated change, no algorithmic impact.

recommendations.description: Q6 scans 60M lineitem rows with 3 numeric filters (shipdate, discount, quantity). Current scalar loop processes 1 row/iteration. Implement AVX2 SIMD to process 8 int32s (dates) or 4 doubles (discount, quantity) per instruction. Implementation: (1) Include <immintrin.h>. (2) Align columns: 'alignas(32) std::vector<int32_t> l_shipdate'. (3) Replace scalar filter loop with: '__m256i start_vec = _mm256_set1_epi32(start_date); for (i = 0; i < n; i += 8) { __m256i dates = _mm256_load_si256(&l_shipdate[i]); __m256i cmp = _mm256_and_si256(_mm256_cmpgt_epi32(dates, start_vec), _mm256_cmpgt_epi32(end_vec, dates)); int mask = _mm256_movemask_epi8(cmp); ... }'. (4) Use selection vector to gather qualifying indices, then process discount/quantity filters on survivors. (5) Handle remainder (<8 elements) with scalar code. Expected throughput: 8 rows/iteration vs 1 row/iteration.
recommendations.expected_improvement: Q6 execution time: 256ms → 80-120ms (2-3x speedup). Date filter evaluates 8 rows per instruction (8x throughput). Memory-bound workload limits speedup to 2-3x (DRAM bandwidth bottleneck). Low risk if fallback to scalar code for remainder.

recommendations.description: Q1 scans 59M lineitem rows and computes aggregates. Current implementation: scalar loop with perfect hash aggregation (already good). Add SIMD: (1) Process shipdate filter with AVX2 (8 dates per comparison, same as Q6). (2) Vectorize arithmetic: 'disc_price = price * (1 - discount)' can be computed for 4 doubles at once with _mm256_mul_pd, _mm256_sub_pd, _mm256_fmadd_pd. (3) Keep perfect hash aggregation (4-6 groups) but feed it with SIMD-computed results. (4) Challenge: returnflag/linestatus are chars - use gather/scatter or process in batches. Implementation: Outer loop processes 8-row chunks with SIMD, inner loop updates aggregates (still array-indexed). Expected: 1.5-2x speedup (memory-bound limit).
recommendations.expected_improvement: Q1 execution time: 271ms → 150-180ms (1.5-1.8x speedup). Closes gap with DuckDB (199ms) to within 10-20%. Moderate risk - complex interaction between SIMD arithmetic and perfect hash aggregation. Recommend: implement simple SIMD date filter first (low risk), then add SIMD arithmetic if profiling shows compute-bound.

recommendations.technique: Right-size hash table reservations based on actual selectivity
recommendations.description: Q3 pre-sizes hash tables with reserve() but uses over-estimates: qualifying_customers.reserve(400000) but only ~300K qualify (20% of 1.5M); qualifying_orders.reserve(1000000) but iteration 2 analysis suggests 350K is correct (46% of 15M × 20% customer selectivity = 690K orders × 50% customer match = 345K). Over-reservation wastes memory (800K unused slots × 24 bytes = 19 MB). Right-size based on selectivity: (1) qualifying_customers.reserve(350000) [1.5M × 0.2 + 10% safety = 330K]. (2) qualifying_orders.reserve(400000) [15M × 0.46 × 0.25 + margin = 360K, round to 400K]. (3) aggregates.reserve(400000) [one group per qualifying order × 1.2 for hash table load factor]. This frees ~20 MB and reduces hash table cache footprint.
recommendations.expected_improvement: Memory savings: 20-30 MB (minor, but helps avoid OOM). Q3 execution time: 0-5% faster (better cache locality for smaller hash tables). Very low risk - simple constant changes. Recommend: measure actual sizes after selective column loading fix, then tune reserves to actual + 20% margin.

recommendations.technique: Replace hash aggregation with sorted aggregation (if data sorted by orderkey)
recommendations.description: Q3 groups by l_orderkey (high cardinality - ~300K groups). Current: hash aggregation (std::unordered_map) with 300K insertions, each with hash computation + bucket lookup + potential resize. Alternative IF lineitem is sorted by l_orderkey: sorted aggregation with single-pass accumulation. Since consecutive rows have same orderkey, aggregate in place: 'if (current_key == prev_key) { agg.revenue += revenue; } else { output previous group; start new group }'. No hash table needed. Memory: 300K hash entries (24-32 bytes each = 7-10 MB) → single accumulator (48 bytes). Execution: O(n log n) sort + O(n) scan vs O(n) hash ops. HOWEVER: lineitem is sorted by l_shipdate (not l_orderkey) per storage_design.json. This optimization ONLY works if we re-sort filtered lineitem by l_orderkey before aggregation. Cost of sort: ~32M rows (after l_shipdate filter) × 12 bytes (orderkey, extendedprice, discount) = 384 MB to sort. Quicksort: O(n log n) ≈ 32M × 25 = 800M comparisons ≈ 200-400ms. Break-even: sort cost (200-400ms) vs hash aggregation cost (50-100ms). VERDICT: Not worth it unless sorting by l_orderkey during load (storage_design change - higher priority for future iterations).
recommendations.expected_improvement: Negative or neutral for this iteration (data not pre-sorted). Potential future optimization if storage layer sorts lineitem by l_orderkey instead of l_shipdate. Mark as 'FUTURE' - defer to iteration 4+.
recommendations.knowledge_source: aggregation/sorted-aggregation.md (pre-sorted aggregation), storage_design.json (current sort order: l_shipdate)

recommendations.technique: Expression reuse - already implemented, verify correctness
recommendations.description: Q1 already implements expression reuse: 'double disc_price = price * (1.0 - discount); double charge = disc_price * (1.0 + tax);' avoids recomputing disc_price. Verify this is working correctly in generated code at q1.cpp lines 44-46. No change needed - this is already optimal. Mention for completeness. Expected savings: 60M multiplications avoided → ~20-30ms saved (already realized).
recommendations.expected_improvement: Already implemented. No further improvement. Verification-only recommendation.
recommendations.knowledge_source: workload_analysis.json (expression reuse opportunity), current code review (already implemented)

recommendations.description: Q6 already orders filters correctly: shipdate (14% selectivity) → discount (20%) → quantity (48%). This is optimal - most selective first minimizes work. Current implementation at q6.cpp lines 32-38 is correct. No change needed. Verification-only.
recommendations.knowledge_source: workload_analysis.json (multi-predicate filter ordering), current code review

storage_changes.change: Implement selective column loading - load only columns required by executing query instead of all 16 columns
storage_changes.rationale: CRITICAL: Eliminates OOM crash. Lineitem has 16 columns but Q1 uses 7, Q3 uses 4, Q6 uses 4. Loading unused columns (especially l_comment VARCHAR(44) - 60M × 50 bytes avg = 3 GB, l_shipinstruct CHAR(25) - 60M × 25 = 1.5 GB, l_shipmode CHAR(10) - 60M × 10 = 600 MB) wastes 5+ GB. Selective loading: Q1 loads 7/16 columns (56% reduction), Q6 loads 4/16 columns (75% reduction). Expected total memory: 20 GB → 4-6 GB.

storage_changes.change: Implement selective column loading - load only columns required by Q3 (o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority) instead of all 9 columns
storage_changes.rationale: Orders has 9 columns but Q3 uses only 4. Unused columns: o_comment (VARCHAR(79) - 15M × 80 bytes avg = 1.2 GB), o_clerk (CHAR(15) - 15M × 15 = 225 MB), o_orderpriority (CHAR(15) - 225 MB). Selective loading saves 1.6+ GB. This is secondary to lineitem (smaller table) but contributes to OOM fix.

storage_changes.change: Implement selective column loading + string interning for c_mktsegment
storage_changes.rationale: Customer has 8 columns but Q3 uses only 2 (c_custkey, c_mktsegment). Unused columns: c_name, c_address, c_phone, c_comment (total ~1.5M × 150 bytes avg = 225 MB). Selective loading saves 225 MB. Additionally, intern c_mktsegment (5 distinct values across 1.5M rows) for 50 MB savings + faster comparisons.

storage_changes.change: Change string columns from std::vector<std::string> to std::vector<const char*> where interned, or keep as std::string but only for loaded columns
storage_changes.rationale: std::string has 24-byte overhead per instance. For 60M lineitem rows with unused string columns, this is 24 × 60M × 3 unused string columns = 4.3 GB overhead alone. Selective loading eliminates this by not creating std::string instances for unneeded columns. For loaded low-cardinality strings (c_mktsegment, o_orderpriority), interning replaces 24-byte std::string + data with 8-byte const char* pointer.

summary: CRITICAL PRIORITY: Fix memory exhaustion crash preventing Q3/Q6 execution by implementing selective column loading (recommendation #1). Current system loads ALL columns (16 for lineitem, 9 for orders, 8 for customer) into memory but queries use only 20-40% of columns. Unused string columns (l_comment, l_shipinstruct, o_comment, c_address, etc.) consume 8-12 GB of 15.7 GB peak memory. Solution: Modify storage loaders to accept column lists, skip loading unused columns. Expected impact: 15.7 GB → 4-6 GB (70% reduction), enables Q3/Q6 to execute. SECONDARY PRIORITIES: (2) String interning for low-cardinality columns (100-200 MB savings, 5-10x faster string comparisons in Q3 customer filter). (3-4) SIMD vectorization for Q6 and Q1 scans (2-3x speedup for date filtering, 1.5-2x for Q1 with vectorized arithmetic). (5) Right-size Q3 hash table reservations (20-30 MB savings, minor cache improvement). DEFER: Sorted aggregation for Q3 (requires storage design change - sort by l_orderkey instead of l_shipdate). VERIFICATION-ONLY: Expression reuse (Q1) and filter ordering (Q6) are already optimal. EXPECTED OUTCOMES after iteration 3: Q1: 271ms → 150-200ms (close to DuckDB 199ms), Q3: CRASH → 1500-2500ms (vs DuckDB 135ms - still 10-18x slower, but FUNCTIONAL), Q6: CRASH → 60-100ms (vs DuckDB 27ms - 2-3x slower). The 10-18x gap on Q3 is acceptable for this iteration - the priority is enabling execution, not optimizing to parity. Future iterations can address Q3 join performance with partitioned hash joins, sorted storage, or compiled execution.
